data.title: Venture Corp. Target Price Lowered to S$12.50 From S$14.70 by RHB Research >V03.SG
Australia's QMines jumps on acquisition of Mount Mackenzie Mines
data.description:
** Shares of QMines rise 6.1% to A$0.0435, their highest level since April 9

** The copper and gold miner will acquire Mount Mackenzie Mines, a unit of Resource and Energy Group

** Acquisition for total consideration of A$2.485 million ($1.58 million), to enhance co's silver and gold exposure

** Stock down 19.4% YTD

($1 = 1.5763 Australian dollars)

data.title: China Industrial Growth Strongest Since 2021
data.description:
China's industrial production rose by 7.7% year-on-year in March 2025, beating market expectations of a 5.6% increase and accelerating from the 5.9% growth recorded in the January–February period.

It was the strongest expansion in industrial output since June 2021, driven by faster growth across all major sectors: manufacturing (7.9% vs. 6.9% in January–February), electricity, heat, gas, and water production and supply (3.5% vs. 1.1%), and mining (9.3% vs. 4.3%).

On a monthly basis, industrial output rose by 0.44% in March.

In the first quarter of 2025, the industrial production grew by 6.5%.

Last year, industrial output grew by 5.8%.

data.title: China Fixed Investment Rises Slightly More than Expected
data.description:
China’s fixed-asset investment grew by 4.2% year-on-year in January-March 2025, slightly higher than market forecasts and the previous month’s 4.1% rise.

Investments in the primary (16%), secondary (11.9%), and tertiary (0.1%) industries all increased.

data.title: China Economy Expands 1.2% QoQ in Q1
data.description:
The Chinese GDP grew by a seasonally adjusted 1.2% in Q1 of 2025, slowing from a 1.6% rise in Q4 and falling short of market consensus of 1.4%.

It marked the softest quarterly expansion since Q2 of 2024 while representing the 11th consecutive quarter of advance.

data.title: China March aluminium output rises 4.4% y/y to 3.75 million tons
data.description:
China's aluminium output rose by 4.4% to 3.75 million metric tons in March from a year earlier, according to data released by the National Bureau of Statistics on Wednesday.

China produced 11.07 million metric tons of aluminium in the first three months of 2025, a rise of 3.2% from the same period last year, the data showed.

Production of 10 nonferrous metals - including copper, aluminium, lead, zinc and nickel – rose 3.7% to 6.92 million metric tons in March from a year earlier.

The total output of 10 metals for the first three months of this year was up 2% year-on-year to 19.85 million metric tons. The other non-ferrous metals are tin, antimony, mercury, magnesium and titanium.

data.title: China Jobless Rate Lower Than Expected
data.description:
China’s surveyed unemployment rate fell to 5.2% in March 2025, easing from a two- year high of 5.4% in the previous month, below the expected 5.3%.

The survey jobless rate for the locally registered labor force was 5.3%, while that of the non-local registered labor force stood at 4.9%, including 5.0% for those with agricultural registration.

The urban unemployment rate across 31 major cities was 5.2%.

Nationwide, employees in enterprises worked an average of 48.5 hours per week.

In the first quarter, the national urban survey unemployment rate averaged 5.3%.

data.title: U.S. Coal Revival Likely to Cloud Sime Darby's OutlookMarket Talk
data.description: Sime Darby's Australasian industrial segment is likely facing rising risks from a shift in U.S. coal policy, Affin Hwang IB analyst Afifah Ishak says in a note. Its Australasian industrial business accounted for nearly half of FY 2024 profit. As the U.S. aims to regain coal export share, Australia may lose competitiveness, posing downside risks for Sime Darby's machinery sales, she says. Affin Hwang maintains a sell rating on Sime Darby, citing sustained challenges across key business lines, including weaker met coal prospects in Australia, auto headwinds in China and foreign selling in Malaysian equities. It keeps its target price at MYR1.86. Shares are 2.3% lower at MYR2.13. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: NZX Midday Sector Update: Energy Minerals Soar, Utilities Tumble
data.description:
Shares of energy minerals firms gained the most on the New Zealand's Exchange, rising past 2% by midday Wednesday.

Channel Infrastructure NZ shares rose past 2% in recent trade after it said on Tuesday that it's "well positioned" to meet any additional stockholding requirements in line with the government's minimum supply obligation for diesel importers.

On the other hand, utilities shares dropped nearly 1%.

Meridian Energy's shares slid almost 3% in recent trade as the company's retail sales volumes declined 3.1% year on year to 786 gigawatt hours (GWh) in March from 811 GWh a year earlier.

data.title: Asian Markets Fall Ahead of China Data
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao

Asian markets declined Wednesday morning ahead of Chinese economic data, following negative signals from U.S. equity futures as investors await further clarity about the Trump administration's trade policy.

The Nikkei Stock Average declined 0.6% to 34064.62, while South Korea's Kospi, another export-driven and tech-heavy economy, fell 0.5% to 2465.64.

Chip and electronic stocks were among the biggest losers on both indexes. South Korea's memory-chip maker SK Hynix, which supplies high-bandwidth-memory chips to Nvidia, fell 3.4%, tracking Nvidia's after-hours decline. In Japan, Advantest lost 4.3% and Nidec Corp. was 2.4% lower.

Nvidia said in a filing that it is anticipating a charge of up to $5.5 billion tied to exporting its artificial-intelligence chips to China.

Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index fell 0.7%, while China's Shanghai Composite Index was down 0.4%.

In another sign of market nerves, spot gold touched an intraday high of $3,275 per ounce on Wednesday morning.

Trepidation ahead of key China economic data is also front of mind as the Asian session gets underway.

President Trump said Tuesday that China will need to come to the U.S. if it wants to negotiate for lower tariffs.

"The ball is in China's court," the president said in a new statement read at the White House press briefing by press secretary Karoline Leavitt. "China needs to make a deal with us, we don't have to make a deal with them."

Earlier this week, the Trump administration paused tariffs on consumer electronics, giving investors a brief reprieve. Stocks broadly advanced across the region, but a lack of clarity on looming tariff developments is weighing on sentiment.

Asian currencies were mixed against the U.S. dollar. The greenback fell 0.2% against the yen, but was flat against the Korean won.

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com

data.title: China Mining Reports Three Consecutive Years of Losses; to be Placed on SGX Watch-List
data.description:
China Mining is set to be put on the SGX Watch-List after reporting three consecutive years of losses, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Tuesday.

The company had an average daily market capitalization of SG$15.1 million in the last six months.

Companies with an average daily market capitalization of less than SG$40 million along with three consecutive years of losses are placed on the watch-list.

data.title: Japan's Nikkei sags as chip shares dragged down by Nvidia curbs
data.description:
Japan's Nikkei share average was headed for its first decline this week on Wednesday, as chip-sector shares sank after Nvidia said the U.S. government is limiting exports of a key chip to China.

Chip-testing equipment maker Advantest - an Nvidia supplier - was the Nikkei's worst performer with a 5% tumble. Chip-making equipment manufacturer Disco fell 4%, and silicon producer Sumco dropped 3.1%.

The tech-heavy Nikkei lost 0.4% to 34,136.70 by mid-morning, while the broader Topix dipped 0.2%.

Precision equipment makers (.IPRCS.T) dropped 1.6% as a sector, making them the worst performer among the Tokyo Stock Exchange's 33 industry groupings.

The H20 artificial intelligence chip targeted by the U.S. government is currently Nvidia's most advanced chip for sale in China, and is central to its efforts to stay engaged with China's booming AI industry.

Nvidia has been a key focus of U.S. export controls as officials attempt to keep the most advanced chips from being sold to China. As a result, Nvidia has been designing chips that come as close as possible to U.S. limits.

"The Nvidia news is a massive development for U.S. tech and tech companies everywhere," said Kyle Rodda, senior financial markets analyst at Capital.com.

"It suggests that whatever innovation the company comes up with to get around U.S. restrictions, policymakers are going to eventually clamp down on it," Rodda said.

"That's going to stifle or limit Nvidia sales growth, market share and overall growth potential."

Outside of chip-sector shares, Japanese equities had little direction as investors awaited additional news on U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff policies.

Of the Nikkei's 225 components, 131 fell versus 90 that rose, with four flat.

U.S.-Japan trade talks are due to get under way this week, with Japan's top negotiator, Ryosei Akazawa, arriving in Washington later on Wednesday for a three-day visit.

data.title: Major Harley-Davidson Shareholder Seeks Board Shake-Up
data.description:
By Lauren Thomas and John Keilman

A major Harley-Davidson shareholder is preparing to launch a fight to shake up the motorcycle maker's board and quickly replace its chief executive in the face of deteriorating sales, according to people familiar with the matter.

The investment firm H Partners, which has a roughly 9% stake in Harley-Davidson, is preparing to urge shareholders to remove three longstanding directors from Harley's eight-member board at the annual meeting in mid-May, the people said.

H Partners is also calling for Jochen Zeitz to step down immediately as Harley's chief executive officer and not stand in the way of the company's recruiting an external replacement, the people added. The firm is focused on the next CEO's ability to connect with customers and dealers, they said.

Harley said last week that it was seeking a new CEO as Zeitz plans to retire after five years on the job. Harley said it retained an executive search firm in late 2024 after Zeitz expressed interest in retiring. Zeitz is expected to remain in his position until a successor is chosen.

H Partners's Jared Dourdeville abruptly resigned from Harley's board earlier this month, arguing in a now-public letter that Harley had suffered "cultural depletion" because of an extensive, white-collar work-from-home policy and the departure of several senior leaders, among other concerns.

"By airing the board's confidential search and publicly campaigning against the company, the board believes Mr. Dourdeville is also adversely impacting the CEO search process," the company said in a statement, adding the campaign would impede execution of its strategy and put shareholders' interests at risk.

Dourdeville, a partner at the firm, joined Harley's board in 2022 as H Partners became what is now Harley's second-biggest shareholder.

Dourdeville said he had been privately calling for the immediate resignation of Zeitz. Harley said that its directors had interviewed three potential CEOs but declined to offer any the role.

Zeitz helped boost Harley's profit during his tenure, but sales of the company's bikes have continued to decline as the brand struggles to resonate with a younger generation of riders.

Zeitz took over in 2020 as the Covid-19 pandemic took hold. He helped keep the Milwaukee-based company running, despite factory closings and supply-chain tangles. Still, complaints have mounted from dealers who have said that the company doesn't offer enough for entry-level riders and that they have been loaded up with inventory that isn't selling.

The company argues that it is holding up better than its competitors in a powersports market hurt by high interest rates and low consumer confidence.

H Partners will run a so-called withhold-the-vote campaign targeting Zeitz, who has been a board member for 18 years; Thomas Linebarger, who has been a board member for 17 years; and Sara Levinson, who has been a board member for 29 years, the people familiar with the matter said. (Harley's bylaws require directors who don't receive more than 50% of votes in an election to tender their resignations.)

The firm isn't nominating replacement directors.

H Partners, based in New York, ran one of the first big withhold-the-vote campaigns in 2015 at the mattress maker Tempur Sealy. The firm has also struck agreements in the past with companies including Six Flags Entertainment and the motor manufacturer Remy International.

Write to Lauren Thomas at lauren.thomas@wsj.com and John Keilman at john.keilman@wsj.com

data.title: South Korean Won Pressured Ahead of Trade Talks
data.description:
The South Korean won held its recent decline around 1,428 per dollar as market sentiment remained cautious ahead of upcoming trade negotiations between the US and key partners, including South Korea.

Finance Minister Choi Sang-mok is scheduled to meet with US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent next week to address trade issues, including the new US import tariffs.

Separately, Trade Minister Cheong In-kyo emphasized South Korea’s goal to strengthen cooperation with the US in shipbuilding and energy, using it as a potential bargaining chip during the 90-day grace period.

However, concerns persisted as the Trump administration aims to pressure over 70 countries to reduce ties with China and has launched a probe into new tariffs on critical mineral imports, further escalating trade tensions.

Meanwhile, the won found some support as South Korea’s import prices fell for the second consecutive month in March, fueling bets that the Bank of Korea may pause its rate-cutting cycle in April.

data.title: South Korea's Export, Import Prices Rise in March
data.description:
South Korea's export price index, measured in Korean won, rose 6.3% year on year in March, while the import price index increased 3.4%, the Bank of Korea reported Wednesday.

On an on-month basis, the export price index and the import price index increased 0.3% and declined 0.4%, respectively, the South Korean central bank said.

The net barter terms of trade index rose 0.8% on-year during the month.

data.title: China Q1  GDP Growth Tops Estimates
data.description:
The Chinese economy expanded by 5.4% yoy in Q1 of 2025, keeping the same pace as in Q4 and exceeding market consensus of 5.1%.

It remained the strongest annual growth rate in 1-1/2 years.

data.title: China March aluminium output rises 4.4% y/y to 3.75 million tons
data.description: China's aluminium output rose by 4.4% to 3.75 million metric tons in March from a year earlier, according to data released by the National Bureau of Statistics on Wednesday.

data.title: China property investment falls 9.9% y/y in January-March
data.description:
Property investment in China fell 9.9% in the first three months of 2025 from a year earlier, after dropping 9.8% in the January-February period, official data showed on Wednesday.

Property sales by floor area were down 3.0% in January-March from the previous year, versus a 5.1% contraction in the first two months.

New construction starts measured by floor area shrank 24.4%, after plunging 29.6% in January-February.

Funds raised by China's property developers declined 3.7%, following a 3.6% drop in the first two months.

data.title: China Posts Better-Than-Expected Growth in First Quarter
data.description:
(02:00 GMT) *China 1Q GDP +5.4% on Year; Market Expected +5.2%
(02:00 GMT) *China 1Q GDP +1.2% on Quarter
(02:00 GMT) *China Jan-Mar GDP +5.4% on Year
(02:00 GMT) China Posts Better-Than-Expected Growth in First Quarter

The Chinese economy began the year with a relatively solid start, helped by Beijing's stimulus. But that may give policymakers little solace as the trade war with the U.S. deepens.

Official data released Wednesday showed that China's gross domestic product expanded 5.4% from a year ago during the January-March quarter. That matched the prior quarter's 5.4% growth and topped the 5.2% rise tipped by a Wall Street Journal survey of economists.

Other monthly economic indicators signaled some improvements in the world's second-largest economy, with consumption and industrial output rebounding above market expectations.

Retail sales--a gauge of consumption--increased 5.9% on the year in March, according to the National Bureau of Statistics. That compared with the 4.0% growth recorded in January-February and the 4.4% increase expected by economists. China combines some data for the first two months of the year to iron out distortions from the timing of the Lunar New Year.

China's fixed-asset investment rose 4.2% on year in the first quarter, compared with the 4.1% rise seen in January-February and the forecast given by the surveyed economists.

Meanwhile, industrial output rose 7.7% on the year last month, compared with the 5.9% increase seen in the first two months of the year and economists' forecasts for 5.7% growth.

China's surveyed urban jobless rate stood at 5.2% in March, down from 5.4% in February.

Many economists had expected Beijing's proactive fiscal policies to have helped boost growth in the first quarter, also seeing support coming from front-loading demand ahead of President Trump's raising of tariffs on China in early April.

As U.S. tariffs start to take a toll on Chinese exports, economists expect Beijing to hand out aggressive stimulus to buttress growth. With monetary easing constrained by concerns around currency stability and banks' thin profit margins, analysts say Beijing could well ramp up government borrowing later this year.

Write to Singapore editors at singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: China Mar Industrial Output +7.7% On Year; Market Expected +5.7%
China Mar Industrial Output +0.44% On Month
China Jan-Mar GDP +5.4% on Year
China Home Prices Fall Again, But at Slightly Slower Pace
data.description:
Home prices in major Chinese cities fell at a slower pace in March, as Beijing continued to grapple with weak domestic demand following the imposition of steep tariffs by the U.S.

Average home prices in the 70 cities surveyed by the government fell 0.1% in March from a month earlier, virtually unchanged a 0.14% month-on-month drop recorded in February, according to calculations done by The Wall Street Journal based on data released Wednesday.

The figures from the National Bureau of Statistics showed that 41 cities reported month-on-month home price declines in March, down from 45 in February.

On an annual basis, average home prices in the major cities dropped 5.0% in March, slightly better than the 5.2% decline recorded in February. Among the 70 cities, 68 reported year-on-year price declines, the same as in February.

China's property sector, along with domestic consumption, has become central to Beijing's efforts to cushion the impact of escalating trade tensions with the U.S.

Focus is on China's first-quarter gross domestic product and other key economic data due later on Wednesday.

Write to Singapore editors at singaporeeditors@dowjones.com
